title: Exposure Units and Source Emissivity
narrationScript: Radiography runs on numbers. Before you can plan an exposure, set a boundary, or trust a survey meter, you need a unit that says how much radiation is present at a point, and you need to know how much radiation your source or your machine actually puts out. That is this lesson. Start with the idea of exposure. When X-rays or gamma rays pass through air, they ionize it, stripping electrons from air molecules and leaving charged ion pairs behind. Exposure is simply the amount of ionization charge produced in a known mass of air. The SI unit is the coulomb per kilogram. One coulomb per kilogram means the photons liberated one coulomb of charge of either sign in one kilogram of dry air. Notice the definition carefully. Exposure is defined only for X-rays and gamma rays, and only in air. It is not the dose to a person and it is not the energy absorbed in steel. It is ionization of air, full stop. The older unit, the roentgen, is the one you will still hear everywhere in industrial radiography in the United States. One roentgen equals two point five eight times ten to the minus four coulombs per kilogram, exactly, by definition. Flip that around and one coulomb per kilogram is nearly four thousand roentgens, so the roentgen is the friendlier size for field work, and survey meters commonly read in milliroentgens per hour. Because it takes on average about thirty four electron volts of deposited energy to create each ion pair in air, the charge collected is a direct stand in for energy delivered by the photon field. That is what makes exposure practical. An ion chamber survey meter is essentially a known mass of air between two electrodes. Radiation ionizes the air, the electrodes sweep up the charge, and the meter converts that current into exposure rate. For photons, an exposure of one roentgen corresponds to roughly one rad of absorbed dose in soft tissue, which is close enough for working estimates. The careful distinctions between exposure, absorbed dose, and dose equivalent belong to your radiation safety course. What you must own here is that the coulomb per kilogram and the roentgen describe ionization in air, and that is exactly what a calibrated survey meter measures. Now emissivity, the output side of the question. For a gamma ray source, emissivity tells you the exposure rate the source produces at a standard distance for each unit of activity. The numbers worth memorizing are these. Iridium one ninety two gives about half a roentgen per hour per curie at one meter. Cobalt sixty gives about one point three roentgens per hour per curie at one meter, roughly two and a half times hotter per curie. Selenium seventy five, the lower energy source used for thin wall and high contrast work, gives roughly zero point two. The arithmetic is direct. An eighty curie iridium source produces about forty roentgens per hour at one meter, unshielded. A forty curie cobalt source produces about fifty two. Treat these constants as good approximations rather than exact physics, because a real source pellet absorbs some of its own radiation, and the capsule construction shaves the output further. And remember that output rides on activity. As your iridium decays through its seventy four day half life, the roentgens per hour at one meter fall in exact proportion to the curies, which is why the decay chart travels with the source. X-ray machines are a different story, because their output is not fixed by nature. It is set by the controls. The tube current in milliamperes sets how many electrons strike the target each second, so it scales the intensity directly. Double the milliamperes and you double the output, with no change in beam quality. The kilovoltage sets how hard the electrons hit, which changes both the penetrating quality of the beam and the intensity, and the intensity climbs faster than the kilovoltage itself, roughly with its square. Filtration, target design, and generator waveform all move the number too, which is why every machine has its own emission chart, and why you consult that chart instead of guessing. To give you a feel for scale, a typical two hundred fifty kilovolt industrial unit running at five milliamperes can deliver on the order of several hundred roentgens per hour at one meter. Compare that with the forty from our eighty curie iridium source. A working X-ray machine can out emit a sizeable field gamma source many times over, so nobody gets to think of machine radiography as the gentle kind. The difference is that the X-ray beam dies the instant the high voltage is off, while the gamma source emits every second of its life, shielded or not. Either way, the number you trust is the one on a calibrated survey meter, not the one in your head.
imagePrompt: A radiographer kneels on a pipeline right-of-way at dawn holding a calibrated ion-chamber survey meter toward a yellow gamma-ray projector staged on the gravel, his breath visible in the cold air.

sections.heading: Exposure: Ionization Measured in Air
sections.body:
Pass a beam of X-rays or gamma rays through air and it leaves a trail of ion pairs. **Exposure** quantifies exactly that: the total electric charge of one sign liberated per unit mass of dry air. The SI unit is the **coulomb per kilogram (C/kg)** — one coulomb of ionization charge produced in one kilogram of dry air.

Mind the boundaries of the definition. Exposure applies **only to X- and gamma radiation** — not alpha, beta, or neutron radiation — and **only in air**. It is not the energy absorbed by a part and it is not the dose to a person; it is a measure of the ionizing power of a photon field at a point in air. Those limits are why the later quantities — absorbed dose (rad, gray) and dose equivalent (rem, sievert) — exist; your radiation safety course treats them fully.

The legacy unit, the **roentgen (R)**, remains the everyday language of industrial radiography in the United States: **1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg** exactly, by definition. One coulomb per kilogram is therefore about 3,876 R — an enormous unit, which is why field instruments read in roentgens or milliroentgens per hour while SI-scaled instruments read in microcoulombs per kilogram or related units.

sections.caption: Exposure defined: photons ionize a known mass of dry air, and the collected charge per kilogram is the exposure in C/kg.

sections.heading: From Charge to Working Numbers
sections.body:
Why does charge in air make a sensible radiation unit? Because creating each ion pair in air costs an average of about **34 eV**, the charge collected is a direct proxy for the energy the photon field deposits. That link makes exposure practical: an **ion chamber** survey meter is essentially a known mass of air between electrodes — radiation ionizes the air, the electrodes sweep up the charge, and the meter converts the current to **exposure rate**, milliroentgens per hour on the classic scale.

Two working conversions to keep in your pocket:

- **1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg** (exact, by definition)
- For X- and gamma rays, an exposure of 1 R corresponds to roughly **1 rad of absorbed dose in soft tissue** — close enough for field estimates, never a substitute for proper dosimetry.

When a procedure, a survey record, or a boundary posting speaks in milliroentgens per hour, it is speaking the language of ionization in air — the same physics, scaled to the field.

sections.heading: Emissivity of Gamma Sources
sections.body:
**Emissivity** — often called the specific gamma-ray constant — answers the planning question: how much exposure rate does a source produce per unit of activity at a standard distance? Anchored at one meter, the field-memory values are:

- **Ir-192: ≈ 0.5 R/h per curie at 1 m**
- **Co-60: ≈ 1.3 R/h per curie at 1 m**
- **Se-75: ≈ 0.2 R/h per curie at 1 m**

The arithmetic is direct. An 80 Ci Ir-192 source produces about 0.5 × 80 = **40 R/h at one meter**, unshielded. A 40 Ci Co-60 source produces about 1.3 × 40 = **52 R/h** — per curie, cobalt is roughly 2.6 times hotter than iridium because its photons carry far more energy.

Treat the constants as **good approximations, not exact physics**. A real source pellet absorbs some of its own emission (self-absorption), and the capsule construction trims the output further, so two sources of identical activity can differ measurably. Remember too that emissivity rides on activity: as Ir-192 decays through its ≈ 74-day half-life, the output at one meter falls in exact proportion to the curies. The decay chart's current activity, multiplied by the emissivity constant, is the starting point for every boundary and exposure calculation.

sections.caption: Field reference for common radiographic gamma sources (values approximate; capsule construction affects output).
Output at one meter: fixed per-curie emissivity for gamma sources versus the control-driven output of an X-ray machine.

sections.heading: Emissivity of X-ray Exposure Devices
sections.body:
A gamma source's output is fixed by nature; an X-ray machine's output is set by its **controls**, which makes 'emissivity' a moving target that each manufacturer documents in an **emission chart** for the specific tube.

- **Tube current (mA)** sets how many electrons cross the tube each second. Output scales **linearly**: double the milliamperes, double the intensity, with no change in beam quality.
- **Kilovoltage (kV)** sets how hard the electrons strike the target. It raises the **penetrating quality** of the beam and raises intensity disproportionately — total output climbs roughly with the **square of the kilovoltage**.
- **Filtration, target material and angle, and generator waveform** all shift the curve — which is why output is quoted per machine, not per physics.

For scale: a typical 250 kV directional unit at 5 mA can deliver on the order of **several hundred roentgens per hour at one meter** — compare that with 40 R/h from an 80 Ci Ir-192 source. A working X-ray machine can out-emit a sizeable field gamma source many times over, a fact that should end any notion that machine radiography is the gentle kind. The compensating mercy is absolute: X-ray output **stops the instant the high voltage is off**, while a gamma source emits every second of its life, shielded or not.

sections.title: The meter is the fact
sections.body: Emissivity constants and emission charts are **planning tools**. The radiation field that actually exists at your boundary is established only by a **calibrated survey meter** in your hand — typical NRC/Agreement-State practice requires a survey after every exposure and on every approach to the exposure device. Plan with the constants; believe the meter.

Source and machine output at one meter

checkQuestions.question: The coulomb per kilogram (C/kg) measures:
options: Ionization charge of one sign liberated per kilogram of dry air by X- or gamma radiation
The activity of a radioactive source
checkQuestions.explanation: Exposure is defined only for photons and only in air: the charge liberated per unit mass of dry air. Absorbed dose and activity are separate quantities with their own units.

checkQuestions.question: The exact relationship between the roentgen and the SI exposure unit is:
checkQuestions.explanation: By definition 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg, which makes 1 C/kg ≈ 3,876 R — and explains why field instruments read in R or mR per hour. (3.7 × 10¹⁰ is the curie in disintegrations per second; 34 eV is the energy per ion pair.)

checkQuestions.question: What is the approximate unshielded exposure rate at 1 m from a 60 Ci Ir-192 source (emissivity ≈ 0.5 R/h per Ci at 1 m)?
checkQuestions.explanation: Exposure rate = emissivity × activity: 0.5 R/h per Ci × 60 Ci = 30 R/h at one meter.

checkQuestions.question: Per curie of activity, how does Co-60 output compare with Ir-192?
options: Co-60 is about half of Ir-192
Co-60 is about 2.6 times higher (≈ 1.3 vs ≈ 0.5 R/h per Ci at 1 m)
checkQuestions.explanation: Cobalt-60's 1.17 and 1.33 MeV photons make it roughly 2.6 times hotter per curie than iridium-192 at one meter — a major reason its handling and shielding demands are heavier.

checkQuestions.question: With kilovoltage held constant, doubling the tube current (mA) of an X-ray machine:
options: Doubles the beam intensity with no change in penetrating quality
Doubles the penetrating power of the beam
Raises intensity and shifts the spectrum to shorter wavelengths
checkQuestions.explanation: Tube current sets how many electrons strike the target, so intensity scales linearly with mA, while beam quality (the energy spectrum) is governed by kV.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics Course §5.3–5.5
ICRU exposure quantity definitions: 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg (reference value)
Published specific gamma-ray constants for Ir-192, Co-60, and Se-75 (approximate field values, source-construction dependent)
